enrichment.short_description: Eleventy (11ty) is a free, open-source static site generator that compiles templates into fast, JavaScript-free HTML.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Eleventy is a free open-source static site generator supporting 13+ template languages with zero-config, fast builds.

features.title: Multiple template languages
features.description: Supports HTML, Markdown, WebC, JavaScript, Liquid, Nunjucks, Handlebars, Mustache, EJS, Haml, Pug, TypeScript, JSX, and MDX in the same project.

features.title: Fast builds
features.description: Compiles large sites quickly, with published benchmarks showing 4,000 Markdown files built in under 2 seconds.

features.title: No client-side JavaScript by default
features.description: Eleventy ships plain HTML output with no required JavaScript framework or client-side runtime.

features.title: Zero-config start
features.description: Works out of the box with no required configuration file, while still offering flexible configuration options.

features.title: Works with existing directory structures
features.description: Designed for incremental adoption into existing projects rather than requiring a specific folder layout.

pros: Free and open source with no vendor lock-in
Fast build performance on large sites
Supports many template languages side by side in one project
Ships no JavaScript to the browser by default
cons: Requires Node.js 18 or newer to run
No official hosted/cloud product; deployment must be configured separately with a host of your choice
As a build tool rather than an application, it has no built-in UI for non-developers

faqs.question: Is Eleventy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Eleventy is open source and free to use.

faqs.question: What template languages does Eleventy support?
faqs.answer: 13+ languages, including Markdown, Liquid, Nunjucks, Handlebars, EJS, Pug, JSX, and MDX.

faqs.question: Does Eleventy require Node.js?
faqs.answer: Yes, Eleventy requires Node.js 18 or newer and can be installed via npm, pnpm, or yarn.

faqs.question: Who maintains Eleventy?
faqs.answer: Eleventy was originally created by Zach Leat and is now part of the Font Awesome organization following an acquisition in September 2024.

use_cases.title: Static blogs and marketing sites
use_cases.description: Compile Markdown and template content into pre-rendered HTML pages without a JavaScript framework.

use_cases.title: Documentation sites
use_cases.description: Generate documentation output from Markdown and templates with fast rebuild times.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Eleventy?
paragraphs: Eleventy, also known as 11ty, is an open-source static site generator that compiles template files into a pre-rendered `_site` output folder. It supports 13+ template languages side by side, including Markdown, Liquid, Nunjucks, Handlebars, and JSX, and ships no client-side JavaScript by default.

The project was originally created by Zach Leat and is now maintained under the Font Awesome organization following a September 2024 acquisition. Eleventy is used across a large number of public GitHub repositories for blogs, documentation, and marketing sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Eleventy is for
paragraphs: Eleventy suits developers who want a fast, framework-agnostic static site generator that can be adopted incrementally into an existing project without requiring a full JavaScript framework stack.

faqs.question: What rendering modes does Nuxt support?
faqs.answer: Server-side rendering (SSR), client-side rendering (CSR), and static-site generation (SSG).

faqs.answer: Vue.js for the frontend, Vite as the bundler, and Nitro as the server engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Nuxt?
paragraphs: Nuxt is an open-source, full-stack framework for Vue.js that provides file-based routing, automatic auto-imports, and multiple rendering modes (SSR, CSR, and SSG). It is built on Vue.js, Vite, and the Nitro server engine.

Nuxt is 100% open source under the MIT License and has a modular architecture with more than 200 community-built modules covering things like content management, image optimization, and authentication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Nuxt is for
paragraphs: Nuxt is aimed at Vue.js developers who want an opinionated, batteries-included framework for building production web applications, from small projects to large-scale enterprise sites.

enrichment.short_description: SvelteKit is a free, open-source framework for building web applications with Svelte, offering routing, SSR, and SSG.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SvelteKit is an open-source, MIT-licensed framework for building Svelte web apps with routing, SSR, SSG, and deployment adapters.

features.title: Adapter-based deployment
features.description: Deploys to Node servers, Cloudflare, Netlify, and Vercel through a configurable adapter system.

features.title: Built-in image optimization
features.description: Includes tooling for optimizing images as part of the build process.

features.title: Page preloading
features.description: Fetches pages before the user navigates to them for faster perceived performance.

features.title: Offline support
features.description: Provides service worker functionality for offline use cases.

features.title: Vite-powered dev experience
features.description: Uses Vite with Svelte plugins for hot module replacement and fast local development.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Flexible deployment via adapters for multiple hosting platforms
Fast development experience powered by Vite
Built-in SSR, SSG, and CSR support
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community than React- or Vue-based frameworks
Requires learning Svelte's compiler-based component model

faqs.question: Is SvelteKit free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, SvelteKit is part of the Svelte ecosystem, which is free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What rendering modes does SvelteKit support?
faqs.answer: Server-side rendering (SSR), client-side rendering (CSR), and static-site generation (SSG).

faqs.question: How does SvelteKit handle deployment?
faqs.answer: Through a configurable adapter system supporting Node servers, Cloudflare, Netlify, and Vercel.

faqs.question: What build tool does SvelteKit use?
faqs.answer: SvelteKit uses Vite for development and building.

use_cases.title: Full-featured web applications
use_cases.description: Build routed, server-rendered applications using Svelte components.

use_cases.title: Statically generated sites
use_cases.description: Prerender pages at build time for static hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SvelteKit?
paragraphs: SvelteKit is a framework for building web applications using Svelte, comparable in role to Next.js for React or Nuxt for Vue. It provides filesystem-based routing, SSR, SSG, and CSR, plus an adapter system for deploying to Node, Cloudflare, Netlify, and Vercel.
It is part of the Svelte project, which is free and open source under the MIT license, and is built on top of Vite for development tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who SvelteKit is for
paragraphs: SvelteKit is for developers who want to build performant web applications with Svelte and need routing, rendering, and deployment handled by a cohesive framework rather than assembled from separate tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Remix?
paragraphs: Remix is an open-source, full-stack JavaScript framework that bundles a server, router, data layer, UI components, and testing tools into a single dependency. It is owned and maintained by Shopify, Inc.

Remix's components are built on web-standard primitives such as EventTarget rather than being exclusively tied to React hooks, and the framework is designed to interoperate with web components and third-party libraries. Remix 3 is currently available as a beta release.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Remix is for
paragraphs: Remix targets developers who want a single, cohesive full-stack framework covering routing, data loading, and UI rendering, from quick prototypes through to production applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Qwik?
paragraphs: Qwik is an open-source web framework built around resumability, a technique that skips traditional hydration so applications are instantly interactive. It uses JSX and functional components, and ships with Vite, Vitest, and Playwright as part of its standard tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Qwik is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Woodpecker CI?
paragraphs: Woodpecker CI is a free, open-source continuous integration and delivery engine that runs each pipeline step inside a Docker container. It was forked from Drone CI's last Apache 2.0-licensed release (0.8) in 2019 after Drone changed to a proprietary license, and has since grown into a distinct project maintained by the Woodpecker Authors community.

Pipelines are described in YAML files stored in a .woodpecker/ directory. Steps reference a Docker image and a list of commands, and plugins are simply Docker images that can be shared across projects for tasks like deployments and notifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Woodpecker CI is for
paragraphs: Woodpecker suits engineering teams that want a lightweight, self-hosted CI/CD engine without a hosted-cloud dependency, particularly teams already comfortable running Docker-based infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Drone CI?

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Concourse CI is a free, open-source, self-hosted CI/CD system built around resources, jobs, and containerized tasks defined in YAML.

features.title: Resource-based pipeline model
features.description: External artifacts like Git repos and Docker images are declared as 'resources' that Concourse automatically monitors for changes.

features.title: Containerized tasks
features.description: Every task runs in an isolated container with a specified image, keeping builds reproducible.

features.title: Visual pipeline UI
features.description: A web UI graphs the pipeline so failed jobs can be traced back to their root cause.

features.title: fly CLI for local testing
features.description: The fly execute command lets you run pipeline task logic locally before committing it.

features.title: Interactive container debugging
features.description: The fly intercept command opens a shell directly inside a running or failed task's container.

pros: Fully open source and free to self-host
Declarative, resource-based model version-controls the entire pipeline
Local CLI testing (fly execute) speeds up pipeline development
Visual pipeline UI aids debugging of complex, multi-stage workflows
cons: No official hosted/cloud offering — requires self-hosting
Resource-type abstraction has a steeper learning curve than plugin-based CI tools
Deployment requires managing systemd, Docker Compose, or manual server setup

faqs.question: Is Concourse CI free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Concourse is open-source software and free to download and self-host.

faqs.question: How does Concourse define pipelines?
faqs.answer: Pipelines are written in YAML and modeled as resources, jobs, and tasks, described as a distributed, continuous Makefile.

faqs.question: Can I test Concourse pipelines locally?
faqs.answer: Yes, the fly CLI includes a fly execute command for running task logic locally before pushing it to the pipeline.

faqs.question: How is Concourse deployed?
faqs.answer: Concourse provides documented installation guides for systemd, Docker Compose, and manual configuration for self-hosted deployment.

use_cases.title: Declarative, containerized CI/CD
use_cases.description: Teams that want their entire pipeline defined as version-controlled YAML resources can adopt Concourse's model.

use_cases.title: Debugging complex multi-stage pipelines
use_cases.description: The visual pipeline graph and fly intercept command help trace failures back to their source.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Concourse CI?
paragraphs: Concourse is an open-source continuous integration and delivery system that models pipelines as resources, jobs, and tasks defined in YAML. Rather than relying on a plugin marketplace, it uses a resource-type abstraction so external artifacts like Git repositories and Docker images are automatically monitored for changes.

Every task in a Concourse pipeline runs inside an isolated container with a specified image. The fly CLI supports local testing of task logic with fly execute and direct debugging of running containers with fly intercept.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Concourse CI is for
paragraphs: Concourse fits teams that want a declarative, container-based CI/CD system they fully control and are willing to self-host, particularly those comfortable working with its resource/job/task model instead of a plugin-driven pipeline builder.

enrichment.short_description: Buildbot is a free, open-source Python framework for automating build, test, and release pipelines across distributed workers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Buildbot is a free, open-source, GPL-licensed Python framework for automating distributed software build and test pipelines.

features.title: Master-worker architecture
features.description: One or more masters coordinate build jobs across workers running on different operating systems.

features.title: Python-based configuration
features.description: Buildbot is configured with a Python script, enabling dynamic pipeline generation and custom components.

features.title: Distributed, parallel job execution
features.description: Jobs are queued and executed in parallel across available worker resources.

features.title: Version control monitoring
features.description: Buildbot watches source repositories for changes and triggers builds automatically.

features.title: Extensive status reporting
features.description: Provides detailed reporting on build and test results.

pros: Fully open source under GPL-2.0 and free to use
Highly customizable via Python configuration scripts
Mature project used by high-profile projects like WebKit and CPython
Cross-platform worker support
cons: Requires Python scripting knowledge to configure, unlike YAML-based competitors
No official hosted/cloud offering — requires self-hosting
Less modern UI compared to newer commercial CI platforms

faqs.question: Is Buildbot free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Buildbot is open-source software licensed under GPL-2.0.

faqs.question: What language is Buildbot written in and configured with?
faqs.answer: Buildbot is written in Python (using Twisted) and is configured via a Python script rather than YAML; it requires Python 3.6+.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Buildbot support?
faqs.answer: Buildbot's master-worker architecture is compatible with all major operating systems.

faqs.question: Who uses Buildbot?
faqs.answer: The project cites use by high-profile projects including WebKit, Python, and Twisted.

use_cases.title: Highly customized build pipelines
use_cases.description: Teams needing pipeline logic beyond what YAML config supports can write custom Python configuration.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams with complex, non-standard build requirements

use_cases.title: Cross-platform build farms
use_cases.description: Projects that need to build and test across many operating systems can distribute jobs across workers.
use_cases.audience: Open-source projects and low-level software teams

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Buildbot?
paragraphs: Buildbot is an open-source framework, written in Python using Twisted, for automating software build, test, and release processes. It uses a master-worker architecture in which one or more masters queue and distribute jobs to workers running on different operating systems, then collect and report the results.
Configuration is done through a Python script rather than a fixed YAML schema, allowing dynamic pipeline generation and custom components. Buildbot is licensed under GPL-2.0 and requires Python 3.6+.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Buildbot is for
paragraphs: Buildbot suits teams that need highly customized, code-driven build automation and are comfortable writing Python to configure their CI system, including maintainers of large open-source projects like WebKit and CPython who already use it.

enrichment.short_description: GoCD is a free, open-source continuous delivery server from Thoughtworks that visualizes complex deployment pipelines end to end.

features.title: Complex workflow modeling

features.title: End-to-end traceability

features.title: Cloud-native integrations
features.description: Works with Kubernetes, Docker, AWS, and other cloud environments.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: An extensible plugin system integrates with many external tools.

pros: Fully open source under the Apache License 2.0
Maintained and sponsored by Thoughtworks
Strong visualization of complex, multi-stage delivery pipelines via the value stream map
Free to self-host with no license cost
cons: No official hosted/cloud offering — requires self-hosting
UI and setup are dated compared to newer SaaS CI/CD tools
Steeper learning curve for modeling complex pipelines

faqs.answer: Yes, GoCD is free, open-source software released under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: Who maintains GoCD?
faqs.answer: GoCD is sponsored and maintained by Thoughtworks.

faqs.question: Does GoCD support cloud-native deployments?
faqs.answer: Yes, GoCD works with Kubernetes, Docker, AWS, and other cloud environments.

faqs.question: Where can I find GoCD documentation?
faqs.answer: Documentation is available at docs.gocd.org, alongside download and getting-started guides on the main site.

use_cases.title: Complex, multi-stage delivery pipelines
use_cases.description: Teams with intricate deployment workflows can model parallel execution and dependencies visually.
use_cases.audience: Release and DevOps engineers managing complex CD pipelines

use_cases.title: End-to-end deployment traceability
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to trace a change from commit to production can use GoCD's value stream map and comparison tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is GoCD?
paragraphs: GoCD is a free, open-source continuous delivery and release automation server sponsored and maintained by Thoughtworks. It is released under the Apache License 2.0 and is designed for self-hosted deployment.

Its value stream map visualizes the entire path to production in a single view, supporting parallel execution and dependency management for complex delivery workflows, with real-time traceability of changes from commit to deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who GoCD is for
paragraphs: GoCD suits teams that manage complex, multi-stage continuous delivery pipelines and want strong end-to-end visualization and traceability from a free, self-hosted, open-source server.

features.title: Free plan

features.title: Multiple execution environments
features.description: Supports Docker, Linux, Windows, Arm, macOS, and self-hosted runners.

features.title: MCP server
features.description: Connects AI tools to CircleCI logs, job metadata, and failure context.

features.title: Rollback pipelines

features.title: Docker layer caching and workflows

features.title: Credit-based paid plans

Client list on the site includes well-known companies such as Google and Okta
cons: Paid plans use a credit-based system that can be harder to predict than flat per-seat pricing
Scale plan requires contacting sales for custom, annually billed pricing
Extra active users on the Performance plan cost $15/month each

faqs.question: Does CircleCI have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan includes up to 6,000 build minutes per month, up to 5 active users, and 30x concurrency.

faqs.question: How much does CircleCI's paid plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Performance plan starts at $15/month and includes 30,000 credits and 5 active users, with additional users at $15/month each.

faqs.question: Does CircleCI offer enterprise pricing?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Scale plan offers custom, annually billed pricing with enterprise controls like SSO and unlimited concurrency.

faqs.question: What environments does CircleCI support?
faqs.answer: CircleCI supports Docker, Linux, Windows, Arm, macOS, and self-hosted runners.

use_cases.title: Small teams starting with CI/CD
use_cases.description: The free plan's 6,000 monthly build minutes and 5 users suit small projects getting started.

use_cases.title: Multi-platform build and release
use_cases.description: Teams needing macOS, Windows, Arm, or GPU builds alongside Linux can use CircleCI's varied execution environments.
use_cases.audience: Teams shipping across multiple OS/architecture targets

features: Largest resource classes, all environments including GPU

long_form_blocks.heading: What is CircleCI?
paragraphs: CircleCI is a continuous integration and continuous delivery platform that automates testing, building, and deployment. It offers a free tier alongside credit-based paid plans, and supports execution environments including Docker, Linux, Windows, Arm, and macOS, as well as self-hosted runners.
The platform includes workflow features like parallelism, Docker layer caching, and rollback pipelines, plus an MCP server that connects AI tools to build logs, job metadata, and failure context.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who CircleCI is for
paragraphs: CircleCI suits teams ranging from small projects using the free tier to larger organizations on the custom-priced Scale plan that need enterprise controls like SSO, unlimited concurrency, and GPU environments.

enrichment.short_description: Semaphore is an open-source, pay-as-you-go CI/CD platform built for coding agents, giving every organization $15 of free usage per month.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Semaphore is an open-source, agent-native CI/CD platform with pay-as-you-go pricing and $15 of free usage every month.

features.description: Includes test reporting and support for monorepo projects.

features.title: Enterprise security controls
features.description: SOC 2 Type II certified, with SAML SSO, role-based access control, and audit logs.

Open source — source code can be reviewed and self-hosted
SOC 2 Type II certification with SSO, RBAC, and audit logs for enterprise needs
Native integration with coding agents like Claude and Codex
cons: Usage-based pricing makes costs less predictable than flat-rate plans
Specific per-minute/per-resource pricing isn't published on the marketing site
Agent-native positioning may be less relevant for teams not using AI coding agents

faqs.answer: Every organization gets $15 of free usage each month; usage-based charges apply for CI execution beyond that.

faqs.answer: Yes, Semaphore is open source and can be self-hosted; the source is available on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Agent-driven CI setup

use_cases.audience: Teams adopting AI coding agents in their workflow

use_cases.title: Usage-based CI without per-seat costs

features: SOC 2 Type II, SAML SSO, RBAC, audit logs

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Semaphore?
paragraphs: Semaphore is a continuous integration and delivery platform, formerly at semaphoreci.com and now at semaphore.io, positioned around agent-native automation — letting coding agents like Claude and Codex configure and trigger CI runs directly.

It uses pay-as-you-go pricing with no per-seat fees; every organization receives $15 of free usage each month before usage-based charges apply. Semaphore is open source and supports self-hosted agents alongside its hosted compute.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Semaphore is for
paragraphs: Semaphore fits teams that want usage-based CI pricing without per-seat costs, and especially teams building workflows around AI coding agents that need CI to be triggerable directly by an agent.

features.title: Test Engine

features.title: Package Registries

Test Engine supports 15+ testing frameworks with flaky-test isolation
Customer case studies report large-scale usage, including Uber running 100,000 concurrent agents
cons: Pro plan is priced per active user ($30/month), which can scale up quickly for larger teams
Enterprise plan requires a 30-user minimum and custom pricing
Free Personal plan is limited to 3 concurrent jobs and 1 user

faqs.question: Is Buildkite free?
faqs.answer: The Personal plan is free forever for a single user with 3 concurrent jobs and 90-day build retention. A 30-day All Access Trial with full features is also available.

faqs.question: How much does Buildkite's Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan costs $30 per active user per month and includes unlimited users, unlimited test executions, and all agent sizes including Mac M4.

faqs.question: What is Buildkite's hybrid architecture?
faqs.answer: Buildkite hosts the orchestration, UI, and API, while build jobs run on agents you host yourself or on Buildkite-hosted Mac/Linux agents.

faqs.question: Does Buildkite support mobile app builds?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Mobile Delivery Cloud provides an environment optimized for iOS and Android deployment.

use_cases.title: Large-scale, self-hosted CI
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to keep build infrastructure and secrets on their own network can run Buildkite agents on-premise or in their own cloud.
use_cases.audience: Large engineering orgs with strict infrastructure/security requirements

use_cases.title: Test flakiness reduction
use_cases.description: Teams with large test suites can use Test Engine to split tests intelligently and isolate flaky tests.
use_cases.audience: QA and engineering teams managing large test suites

features: All agent sizes including Mac M4

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Buildkite?
paragraphs: Buildkite is a CI/CD platform built around a hybrid architecture: Buildkite hosts the pipeline orchestration, UI, and API, while build jobs execute on agents you run yourself (or on Buildkite-hosted Mac and Linux agents). This keeps source code and secrets on infrastructure you control.

The platform includes dynamic pipelines with conditional logic and trigger steps, a Test Engine for intelligent test splitting and flaky test isolation across 15+ testing frameworks, Package Registries for artifact management, and a Mobile Delivery Cloud for iOS/Android builds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Buildkite is for
paragraphs: Buildkite suits engineering teams — from solo developers on the free Personal plan to large organizations like Uber and Reddit cited in its case studies — that want CI orchestration as a service while keeping build execution on their own agents.

enrichment.short_description: Buddy is a DevOps platform combining CI/CD pipelines, deployment automation, environment provisioning, and visual testing in one suite.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Buddy combines CI/CD pipelines, deployment automation, environments, and visual testing, with a free plan and plans from €29/month.

features.title: Pipelines

Supports pipeline design via either a visual UI or YAML
SOC 2 certified

faqs.question: How much does Buddy's Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan costs €29/month and includes 2 seats and 1 concurrent pipeline, with extra seats and concurrency billed separately.

faqs.question: What testing frameworks does Buddy's Visual Reviews support?

faqs.question: What operating systems can Buddy pipelines run on?
faqs.answer: Pipelines can run on Intel, ARM, Linux, Windows, Mac, or NixOS.

use_cases.description: Teams that want pipelines, deployments, and environment provisioning in one product rather than stitching together separate tools.

use_cases.title: Visual regression testing in CI
use_cases.description: Teams can add browser/viewport snapshot testing directly into their pipelines using Visual Reviews.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Buddy?
paragraphs: Buddy is a DevOps and platform engineering suite that combines CI/CD pipelines, remote deployment automation, environment provisioning, and visual testing into one product. Pipelines can be triggered by GitHub, AWS, Slack, or server events and designed either visually or in YAML, running across Intel, ARM, Linux, Windows, Mac, or NixOS.

Beyond pipelines, Buddy includes Environments for automated dev/staging/production provisioning, Visual Reviews for browser and viewport snapshot testing with Playwright, Selenium, and Cypress, Tunnels for secure external connectivity, and Domains for multi-tenant DNS/SSL management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Buddy is for
paragraphs: Buddy suits teams that want CI/CD, deployment automation, environment provisioning, and visual testing consolidated in one platform rather than assembled from separate tools, from small teams on the free plan to larger teams on Pro or Hyper.

faqs.answer: Modules include Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery & GitOps, Internal Developer Portal, Infrastructure as Code Management, Feature Management & Experimentation, AI Test Automation, AI SRE, security testing, and cloud/AI cost management.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Harness?
paragraphs: Harness is a software delivery platform covering the lifecycle from code to production, including Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery & GitOps, an Internal Developer Portal, feature flag management, AI-assisted testing, security testing, and cloud/AI cost management. Harness offers a Free plan for individual developers and small teams, plus quote-only Essentials and Enterprise plans for larger organizations. The company also owns and maintains Drone CI, which it acquired in 2020.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Harness is for
paragraphs: Harness suits organizations that want a single modular platform spanning CI, CD, feature flags, security, and cost management rather than assembling separate point tools, from individual developers on the free tier to enterprises negotiating custom Essentials or Enterprise contracts.

enrichment.short_description: Flux is a free, open-source, CNCF Graduated GitOps tool that syncs Kubernetes clusters with the state declared in Git.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flux is a free, open-source CNCF Graduated GitOps tool for Kubernetes, syncing clusters with Git and supporting Helm, Kustomize, and Flagger.

features.title: GitOps synchronization
features.description: Declarative infrastructure and application state stored in Git is automatically and continuously applied to Kubernetes clusters.

features.title: Progressive delivery
features.description: Integrates with Flagger for canary releases, feature flags, and A/B testing on top of GitOps-managed deployments.

features.title: Multi-cluster management
features.description: Manages applications and configuration across multiple Kubernetes clusters from Git.

features.title: Automated image updates

features.title: Pull-based security model
features.description: Uses a pull-based architecture with RBAC and least-privilege principles rather than exposing cluster credentials to CI systems.

features.title: Broad ecosystem support

pros: Free and fully open source
CNCF Graduated project status signals maturity and broad governance
Pull-based architecture reduces the security exposure of cluster credentials
Integrates with Flagger for progressive delivery (canary, A/B testing)
Supports multiple Git providers and Kubernetes distributions
cons: Requires working knowledge of Kubernetes and GitOps concepts to configure
No official managed/hosted offering — you operate it yourself
Progressive delivery features require installing the separate Flagger project

faqs.question: Is Flux free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flux is fully open source and free to use.

faqs.question: Is Flux a CNCF project?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flux is a CNCF Graduated project.

faqs.question: Who created Flux?
faqs.answer: Flux was originally created by Weaveworks and is now developed as a community-driven project under CNCF governance.

faqs.question: Does Flux support canary deployments?
faqs.answer: Yes, through integration with Flagger, a separate progressive delivery tool in the Flux ecosystem.

faqs.question: Which Git providers does Flux support?
faqs.answer: GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and S3-compatible buckets.

use_cases.title: GitOps continuous delivery
use_cases.description: Automatically sync Kubernetes cluster state with a Git repository as the single source of truth.

use_cases.title: Multi-cluster application rollout
use_cases.description: Manage configuration and deployments consistently across multiple Kubernetes clusters.
use_cases.audience: DevOps teams operating fleets of clusters

use_cases.title: Automated image patching
use_cases.description: Automatically detect new container image versions and update Git manifests without manual intervention.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Flux?
paragraphs: Flux is a set of open-source, CNCF Graduated continuous and progressive delivery tools for Kubernetes. It implements GitOps: the desired state of applications and infrastructure is declared in a Git repository, and Flux continuously reconciles the live cluster to match it.

Originally created by Weaveworks, Flux is now a community-driven project governed under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation, with support for Kustomize, Helm, multiple Git providers, and integration with Flagger for progressive delivery such as canary releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Flux is for
paragraphs: Flux is built for platform and DevOps teams running Kubernetes who want to manage cluster state declaratively through Git rather than manual kubectl or CI-driven deployments, particularly teams operating multiple clusters or requiring auditable, pull-based deployment security.

enrichment.short_description: Rancher is SUSE's open-source platform for managing Kubernetes clusters across cloud, on-prem, and edge infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Permify free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Permify's core authorization engine is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license and can be self-hosted for free. A paid, managed Permify Cloud offering is also available.

faqs.question: Who maintains Permify?
faqs.answer: Permify was acquired by FusionAuth in November 2025. FusionAuth has stated Permify remains open source and continues to be developed as a standalone authorization engine.

faqs.question: What authorization models does Permify support?
faqs.answer: Permify supports role-based access control (RBAC), attribute-based access control (ABAC), and relationship-based access control (ReBAC) within a single schema.

faqs.question: Can Permify run in the cloud?
faqs.answer: Yes. Permify Cloud is a managed hosting option with SOC2 and GDPR compliance, available across AWS, GCP, and Azure regions.

use_cases.title: Fine-grained permissions for SaaS applications
use_cases.description: Teams building multi-tenant SaaS products use Permify to centralize resource-level and role-based permission checks instead of hardcoding authorization logic into each service.

use_cases.title: Replacing ad hoc authorization code

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Permify?
paragraphs: Permify is an open-source authorization-as-a-service platform that helps developers build and manage fine-grained, scalable access control systems, inspired by Google's Zanzibar authorization model.

It stores permission data as structured relationships and exposes a check API for evaluating access decisions, supporting RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC models in one schema. Permify was acquired by FusionAuth in November 2025 and remains open source under AGPL-3.0.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Permify is for
paragraphs: Permify suits engineering teams that need centralized, auditable authorization across multiple applications or services rather than reimplementing permission logic in each codebase.

faqs.answer: Casbin supports ACL, RBAC, ABAC, and other hybrid access control models defined through its PERM metamodel configuration files.

use_cases.title: Standardizing access control across microservices

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Casbin?
paragraphs: Apache Casbin is an open-source access control library that supports multiple authorization models, including ACL, RBAC, and ABAC, through a declarative PERM metamodel configuration.

It is implemented across more than a dozen programming languages and can persist policies in a range of databases via pluggable adapters. The project is currently undergoing incubation at the Apache Software Foundation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Casbin is for
paragraphs: Casbin is aimed at developers who need to embed flexible, model-driven authorization directly into an application or service rather than relying on an external authorization API.

cons: Rego is a domain-specific language with its own learning curve distinct from general-purpose programming languages
No official managed cloud offering directly from the OPA project; commercial support is available through third parties such as Styra

faqs.question: Is Open Policy Agent free?
faqs.answer: Yes. OPA is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0 with no paid tier from the project itself.

faqs.question: What is Rego?
faqs.answer: Rego is the declarative policy language used to write OPA policies, designed for expressing rules over structured data such as JSON.

faqs.question: Is OPA a CNCF project?
faqs.answer: Yes. OPA was accepted into the CNCF in March 2018 and reached Graduated maturity status in January 2021.

faqs.question: Can OPA enforce policy in Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, via OPA Gatekeeper, a related project that runs OPA policies as Kubernetes admission controllers.

use_cases.title: Kubernetes admission control
use_cases.description: Platform teams use OPA Gatekeeper to enforce policies on Kubernetes resources at admission time, blocking non-compliant deployments.

use_cases.title: Application-level authorization
use_cases.description: Engineering teams call OPA as a policy decision point to centralize authorization logic across microservices, decoupling it from application code.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Open Policy Agent?
paragraphs: Open Policy Agent (OPA) is an open-source, general-purpose policy engine that lets organizations define, manage, and enforce policies as code using the Rego language.
OPA is a graduated project of the Cloud Native Computing Foundation and is used to enforce policy across microservices, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, and API gateways.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Open Policy Agent is for
paragraphs: OPA is built for platform, security, and DevOps teams that need a unified, code-based way to enforce authorization and compliance policies across heterogeneous infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Conjur Open Source is CyberArk's secrets management and machine-identity platform for securing credentials in cloud-native environments.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Conjur Open Source: CyberArk's secrets management and RBAC engine for securing machine identities and credentials in DevOps pipelines.

features.title: Centralized secret store
features.description: Provides a REST-based secret store with a role-based access control engine, defined using its Machine Authorization Markup Language (MAML).

features.title: Machine identity authentication
features.description: Includes built-in authenticators for Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS IAM, and OIDC to let workloads fetch secrets without static credentials.

features.title: Secretless Broker
features.description: Lets applications connect to backend services without ever fetching or managing passwords and keys directly.

features.title: DevOps toolchain integrations
features.description: Integrates with Ansible, Jenkins, Puppet, and Terraform, as well as CI/CD and container orchestration platforms.

features.title: Multi-language client SDKs
features.description: Official client libraries are available for .NET, Go, Java, Python, and Ruby.

pros: Conjur server is licensed under GNU LGPL v3.0, with client libraries under Apache License 2.0
Actively maintained on GitHub, with releases as recent as mid-2026 and over 900 GitHub stars
Built-in authenticators cover major cloud and container platforms (Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS IAM, OIDC)
Backed by CyberArk, an established identity security vendor, with commercial licenses and support available
cons: CyberArk was acquired by Palo Alto Networks, and the conjur.org marketing domain now redirects to Palo Alto Networks' secrets management product page, which can make it harder to find dedicated Conjur Open Source resources
The full-featured enterprise edition and commercial support require a paid CyberArk/Palo Alto Networks license
Configuration uses a proprietary policy language (MAML) rather than a more widely known format

faqs.question: Is Conjur open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Conjur server is licensed under GNU LGPL v3.0 and client libraries under Apache License 2.0. Commercial licenses are also available from CyberArk.

faqs.question: What does Conjur do?
faqs.answer: Conjur is a secrets management and machine-identity platform that stores secrets, authenticates workloads, and enforces role-based access to credentials.

faqs.question: Which platforms can authenticate to Conjur?
faqs.answer: Conjur includes built-in authenticators for Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS IAM, and OIDC, among others.

faqs.question: Who maintains Conjur today?
faqs.answer: Conjur is maintained by CyberArk, which was acquired by Palo Alto Networks; the open-source project remains actively developed on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Securing Kubernetes workload secrets
use_cases.description: Platform teams use Conjur's Kubernetes authenticator to let pods retrieve secrets dynamically instead of storing credentials in manifests or environment variables.

use_cases.title: CI/CD pipeline credential management
use_cases.description: Organizations integrate Conjur with Jenkins, Ansible, or Terraform so pipelines fetch secrets at runtime rather than hardcoding them.

features: Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS IAM, OIDC authenticators

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Conjur?
paragraphs: Conjur is a secrets management and application-identity platform originally built by CyberArk. It provides a centralized secret store, a role-based access policy engine, and authenticators that let machines and CI/CD pipelines retrieve credentials securely.
The Conjur server is open source under GNU LGPL v3.0, with commercial licenses and enterprise support available from CyberArk, now part of Palo Alto Networks following its 2025 acquisition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Conjur is for
paragraphs: Conjur is built for DevOps and security teams running containerized or cloud-native infrastructure who need to eliminate hardcoded secrets and manage machine identities at scale.

enrichment.short_description: TheHive is a collaborative security incident response and case management platform for SOCs, CSIRTs, and CERTs, developed by StrangeBee.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TheHive: collaborative security case management platform for incident response, with a free Community edition and paid Gold/Platinum tiers.

features.title: Case and alert management
features.description: Organizes SOC work around Cases, Tasks, and Observables, letting teams triage alerts and track incident-response activity.

features.title: Observable analysis via Cortex
features.description: Integrates with Cortex, StrangeBee's analysis and active-response engine, to automatically enrich and analyze indicators such as IPs, hashes, and URLs.

features.title: MISP integration
features.description: Connects to MISP threat intelligence servers to import and correlate indicators of compromise directly within cases.

features.title: 300+ integrations
features.description: StrangeBee's site lists 300+ integrations for pulling alerts from other security tools into TheHive.

features.title: Multiple deployment options
features.description: Available as a self-hosted on-premises install, prebuilt cloud images for AWS/Azure, or a fully managed TheHive Cloud Platform SaaS.

pros: Free Community edition is available for self-hosting, supporting up to 2 users and 1 organization
Deep native integration with Cortex and MISP for threat intelligence and observable analysis
Used by a large community: StrangeBee cites 3,500+ users across 50+ countries
Flexible deployment: self-hosted, cloud images, or managed SaaS
cons: Since StrangeBee took over development in 2018, TheHive 4 and 5 no longer accept external code contributions, and TheHive's earlier fully open-source versions (3 and 4) were discontinued and their repositories archived in 2023
The free Community edition is capped at 2 users and 1 organization; larger teams need a paid Gold or Platinum license
Gold and Platinum plan pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting StrangeBee
Advanced support (business-hours or priority response) is only available on paid tiers

faqs.question: Is TheHive free?
faqs.answer: TheHive offers a free Community edition limited to 2 users and 1 organization. Paid Gold and Platinum editions remove those limits and add support and enterprise features.

faqs.question: What is Cortex?
faqs.answer: Cortex is StrangeBee's companion observable-analysis and active-response engine that TheHive uses to automatically enrich indicators of compromise.

faqs.question: Who develops TheHive?
faqs.answer: TheHive has been developed by StrangeBee since 2018. The current versions (4 and 5) are developed exclusively by StrangeBee's team rather than the open community.

faqs.question: Can TheHive integrate with MISP?
faqs.answer: Yes, TheHive natively integrates with MISP threat intelligence servers to import and correlate indicators.

use_cases.title: SOC alert triage and case management
use_cases.description: Security operations centers use TheHive to centralize alerts from multiple sources into cases, assign tasks, and track investigation progress.

use_cases.title: Incident response for CSIRTs/CERTs
use_cases.description: Incident response teams use TheHive's case and observable model, combined with Cortex analyzers, to investigate and respond to security incidents collaboratively.

features: Business-hours EU support via email, chat, and video, 1-business-day response

long_form_blocks.heading: What is TheHive?
paragraphs: TheHive is a collaborative security case management platform used by SOCs, CSIRTs, and CERTs to manage alerts, cases, and incident-response tasks in one place.

It is developed by StrangeBee and integrates tightly with Cortex for observable analysis and MISP for threat intelligence. A free Community edition remains available alongside paid Gold and Platinum plans and a managed cloud platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who TheHive is for
paragraphs: TheHive is aimed at security operations centers, incident-response teams, and MSSPs that need a structured, collaborative way to manage security cases and investigations.

enrichment.short_description: MISP is a free, open-source threat intelligence platform for collecting, correlating, and sharing indicators of compromise among security teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MISP: free, open-source threat intelligence platform for storing, correlating, and sharing IOCs via STIX, OpenIOC, and TAXII.

features.title: Structured indicator storage
features.description: Stores threat indicators in a structured format that enables correlation, automated export for IDS/SIEM systems, and analysis.

features.title: STIX and OpenIOC export
features.description: Exports threat data in open standard formats including STIX and OpenIOC for interoperability with other security tools.

features.title: Taxonomies and Galaxy classification
features.description: Includes classification systems such as MITRE ATT&CK and Exploit-Kit taxonomies to tag and categorize threat data.

features.title: Community sync and sharing
features.description: Supports multi-instance synchronization so organizations can share threat intelligence across trusted communities.

features.title: PyMISP API
features.description: Provides a REST API and the PyMISP Python library for programmatic access and automation.

pros: Completely free to use, with the software, data format, and API released under open standards and OSI-approved open source licenses
Governed by an interlocked contributor license so no single organization can change MISP's open-source model
Large, active community of users creating and maintaining shared threat-intelligence communities
Broad interoperability via STIX, OpenIOC, and TAXII export formats
cons: Self-hosting and configuring MISP requires security and infrastructure expertise
No official managed SaaS hosting from the MISP project itself; commercial hosting/support comes from third parties
Effective threat-intel sharing depends on joining or building trusted communities, which takes ongoing effort

faqs.question: Is MISP free?
faqs.answer: Yes. MISP is free and open source software, with its data format and API also released as open standards.

faqs.question: What formats does MISP export to?
faqs.answer: MISP supports export to STIX, OpenIOC, and other formats, plus TAXII push for automated distribution to IDS or SIEM systems.

faqs.question: Who uses MISP?
faqs.answer: MISP is used by incident analysts, security and ICT professionals, and malware reversers who need structured threat information sharing.

faqs.question: Can I run my own MISP community?
faqs.answer: Yes. MISP supports multi-instance synchronization, allowing organizations to build and operate their own sharing communities.

use_cases.title: Threat intelligence sharing between organizations
use_cases.description: Security teams use MISP to share indicators of compromise with trusted partners or sector-specific ISACs through synchronized MISP instances.

use_cases.title: Feeding IOCs into SIEM/IDS
use_cases.description: Analysts export structured indicators from MISP in STIX or OpenIOC format to automatically populate detection rules in SIEM and IDS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MISP?
paragraphs: MISP is an open-source threat intelligence platform for collecting, storing, correlating, and sharing indicators related to targeted attacks, financial fraud, and other cyber threats.
It is free to use, with an open data format and API, and is maintained by a large community that operates shared threat-intelligence communities on top of the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MISP is for
paragraphs: MISP is built for incident analysts, security operations teams, and threat intelligence practitioners who need to structure, correlate, and share IOCs across organizations.

enrichment.short_description: OpenCTI is an open-source cyber threat intelligence platform by Filigran for structuring, analyzing, and sharing CTI knowledge.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenCTI: open-source cyber threat intelligence platform from Filigran, with a free Community edition and paid Enterprise Edition.

features.title: STIX2-based knowledge graph
features.description: Structures threat intelligence as a knowledge graph, letting analysts link threat actors, malware, campaigns, and indicators.

features.title: 230+ connectors
features.description: Filigran's site cites 230+ one-click connectors for integrating OpenCTI with other tools across the security stack.

features.title: Enterprise Edition add-ons
features.description: OpenCTI Enterprise Edition adds professional support and additional capabilities on top of the open-source Community Edition.

features.title: Dashboards and enrichment
features.description: Provides dashboards and automated enrichment workflows for turning raw threat data into actionable intelligence.

features.title: Part of the XTM platform
features.description: OpenCTI is positioned by Filigran as part of a broader eXtended Threat Management (XTM) product suite.

pros: OpenCTI Community Edition is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0
Large connector ecosystem (230+) for integrating with other security tools
Backed by Filigran, a dedicated cybersecurity company, with professional training (Filigran Academy) and support
Used by a stated 6,000+ cybersecurity practitioners across individuals, enterprises, and government organizations
cons: Enterprise Edition features are governed by a separate, non-open-source license and require a paid subscription
Enterprise Edition and SaaS pricing are not publicly listed and require contacting Filigran
Running a knowledge-graph-based CTI platform at scale requires infrastructure and analyst expertise

faqs.question: Is OpenCTI free?
faqs.answer: The OpenCTI Community Edition is free and open source under the Apache License 2.0. A paid OpenCTI Enterprise Edition adds additional features and support.

faqs.question: Who develops OpenCTI?
faqs.answer: OpenCTI is developed by Filigran, a cybersecurity company.

faqs.question: How many integrations does OpenCTI support?
faqs.answer: Filigran's site states OpenCTI offers 230+ one-click connectors to integrate with other security tools.

faqs.question: What data model does OpenCTI use?
faqs.answer: OpenCTI structures threat intelligence using the STIX2 standard within a knowledge graph.

use_cases.title: Centralizing threat intelligence analysis
use_cases.description: CTI teams use OpenCTI's knowledge graph to link threat actors, malware, and indicators, turning scattered reports into structured, actionable intelligence.

use_cases.title: Automating intel enrichment via connectors
use_cases.description: Security teams use OpenCTI's connector ecosystem to automatically pull and enrich data from feeds, sandboxes, and other tools into a single platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenCTI?
paragraphs: OpenCTI is an open-source cyber threat intelligence platform that organizes threat data as a STIX2-based knowledge graph, helping analysts structure, analyze, and disseminate actionable intelligence.
It is developed by Filigran, which offers a free Apache 2.0-licensed Community Edition alongside a paid Enterprise Edition with additional features and support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenCTI is for
paragraphs: OpenCTI is aimed at threat intelligence teams, SOCs, and MSSPs that need to structure and correlate CTI data from many sources rather than tracking it in spreadsheets or disconnected tools.

enrichment.short_description: Velociraptor is a free, open-source digital forensics and incident response tool for endpoint monitoring and threat hunting.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Velociraptor: free, open-source DFIR tool for endpoint monitoring, evidence collection, and threat hunting, maintained by Rapid7.

features.title: Velociraptor Query Language (VQL)
features.description: Investigators write custom VQL queries to build targeted hunts and forensic collections tailored to specific investigations.

features.title: Targeted evidence collection
features.description: Collects forensic evidence simultaneously across multiple endpoints for incident response.

features.title: Continuous endpoint monitoring
features.description: Monitors endpoint events such as logs, file changes, and processes, with centralized, indefinite storage of that event data.

features.title: Built-in artifact library
features.description: Ships with a library of forensic artifacts that can be used directly or customized for proactive threat hunting.

features.title: Large-scale hunts
features.description: Designed to run hunts across large deployments, with reported use against tens of thousands of endpoints in real incident response operations.

pros: Fully open source under the AGPL license and free to use
Powerful custom query language (VQL) for building tailored forensic collections and hunts
Maintained by Rapid7, which acquired the Velociraptor team in 2021, providing ongoing development resources
Scales to large, distributed endpoint fleets for enterprise-wide incident response
cons: Rapid7 also integrates Velociraptor into its own commercial InsightIDR/XDR products, which are separate paid offerings from the free open-source tool
VQL has a learning curve for analysts unfamiliar with its query syntax
Requires self-hosting and operational management; there is no vendor-run SaaS version of the open-source tool itself

faqs.question: Is Velociraptor free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Velociraptor is open source under the AGPL license and free to use.

faqs.question: Who maintains Velociraptor?
faqs.answer: Velociraptor is maintained by Rapid7, which acquired the Velociraptor team in 2021, though the tool remains free and open source.

faqs.question: What is VQL?
faqs.answer: VQL (Velociraptor Query Language) is the query language used to write custom forensic collections and threat-hunting queries in Velociraptor.

faqs.question: What platforms does Velociraptor support?
faqs.answer: Velociraptor is used for endpoint monitoring and forensics across large, distributed fleets of endpoints in incident response operations.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-wide incident response
use_cases.description: IR teams deploy Velociraptor across thousands of endpoints to simultaneously collect forensic evidence during active security incidents.

use_cases.title: Proactive threat hunting
use_cases.description: Security teams use Velociraptor's built-in artifact library and VQL to hunt for indicators of compromise across the endpoint fleet before an incident is confirmed.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Velociraptor?
paragraphs: Velociraptor is an open-source digital forensics and incident response (DFIR) tool that gives security teams visibility into endpoints through targeted evidence collection, continuous monitoring, and proactive threat hunting.

It uses its own query language, VQL, to build custom collections and hunts, and is designed to operate across large, distributed endpoint fleets. The project is maintained by Rapid7, which acquired the Velociraptor team in 2021.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Velociraptor is for
paragraphs: Velociraptor is aimed at incident responders and threat hunters who need deep, queryable visibility into endpoint activity across large organizations.

enrichment.short_description: Fleet (FleetDM) is an open-source device management platform built on osquery for managing macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fleet (FleetDM): open-source device management platform for macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android, free and premium tiers.

features.title: Cross-platform device inventory
features.description: Manages device inventory across macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android from a single console.

features.title: Real-time device reporting
features.description: Fleet's site states devices can report data back in under 30 seconds.

features.title: Patch and vulnerability management
features.description: Provides software update and patch management alongside vulnerability visibility across managed devices.

features.title: Infrastructure as code
features.description: Device configuration and policies can be managed as code, and automated via a single REST API.

features.title: Cloud or self-hosted deployment
features.description: Can be run as a self-hosted open-source deployment or as a hosted cloud service.

features.title: Live queries and diagnostics
features.description: Supports live reports, scripting, and diagnostics against endpoints, built on the osquery agent.

pros: Free, open-source version is available and stated to always remain free
Manages a wide range of operating systems (macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, Android) from one console
Fleet states it is used by 1,300+ organizations across 90+ countries managing 2,000,000+ devices
Offers both self-hosted and cloud deployment options
Single API supports automation and infrastructure-as-code workflows
cons: Premium tier pricing is not publicly detailed on the marketing site and requires contacting sales
Advanced features (beyond the free/open-source tier) require a paid premium license
Self-hosting requires infrastructure and operational overhead compared to a fully managed MDM

faqs.question: Is Fleet free?
faqs.answer: Fleet maintains a free, open-source version that the company states will always be free, alongside paid premium tiers with additional features.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Fleet manage?
faqs.answer: Fleet can manage macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android devices from a single console.

faqs.question: Can Fleet be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fleet can be deployed self-hosted or used as a hosted cloud service.

faqs.question: How many organizations use Fleet?
faqs.answer: Fleet's site states it is used by 1,300+ organizations across 90+ countries, managing over 2,000,000 devices.

use_cases.title: Managing heterogeneous device fleets
use_cases.description: IT and security teams use Fleet to inventory and manage devices across multiple operating systems, including subsidiaries or acquired companies with mixed environments.

use_cases.title: Patch and vulnerability management at scale
use_cases.description: Organizations use Fleet's patch management and reporting to track and remediate vulnerabilities across large device fleets via a single API.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Fleet (FleetDM)?
paragraphs: Fleet is an open-source device management platform, built on osquery, that provides inventory, patch management, vulnerability visibility, and automation across macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android devices from a single console.
It offers a free, open-source tier alongside premium plans, and can be deployed self-hosted or as a hosted cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Fleet is for
paragraphs: Fleet is aimed at IT and security teams managing large, mixed-OS device fleets who want open, API-driven device management rather than a closed proprietary MDM.

enrichment.short_description: osquery is a free, open-source tool that exposes an operating system as a relational database for SQL-based security monitoring.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: osquery: free, open-source tool that exposes OS state as SQL tables for security monitoring across Linux, macOS, and Windows.

features.title: SQL-based OS instrumentation
features.description: Exposes operating system information such as running processes, network connections, and file hashes as queryable SQL tables.

features.title: 100+ built-in tables
features.description: Ships with an extensive schema of pre-built tables representing operating system concepts across supported platforms.

features.title: Ad hoc and scheduled queries
features.description: Supports interactive querying through the osqueryi shell and continuous scheduled monitoring via the osqueryd daemon.

features.title: Thrift API extensions
features.description: Provides Thrift-based APIs for building custom applications and extensions on top of the query engine.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

pros: Free and open source, dual-licensed under Apache License 2.0 and GPL 2.0
Governed under an open foundation model (the osquery project moved to the Linux Foundation), providing vendor-neutral governance
Widely adopted, with 23.4k+ GitHub stars and 2.6k+ forks at the time of research
Large, extensible schema (100+ tables) covering processes, network, kernel, and file system data
Regular release cadence, with minor releases roughly every two months
cons: Requires SQL and systems knowledge to write effective queries and schedules
No vendor-run managed service from the osquery project itself; fleet management typically requires a separate tool such as Fleet
Deploying and tuning scheduled queries at scale requires ongoing operational effort

faqs.question: Is osquery free?
faqs.answer: Yes. osquery is free and open source, dual-licensed under Apache License 2.0 and GPL 2.0.

faqs.question: What platforms does osquery support?
faqs.answer: osquery supports Linux, macOS, and Windows.

faqs.question: How do I query with osquery?
faqs.answer: osquery can be queried ad hoc via the osqueryi interactive shell, or scheduled continuously via the osqueryd daemon.

faqs.question: Who governs the osquery project?
faqs.answer: osquery is a community-driven open-source project with GitHub-based issue tracking and a regular release cadence.

use_cases.title: Endpoint security monitoring
use_cases.description: Security teams schedule osquery queries to continuously monitor processes, network connections, and file changes across a fleet of endpoints.

use_cases.title: Incident response investigation
use_cases.description: Responders run ad hoc osquery queries via osqueryi to quickly inspect the state of a compromised or suspicious host.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is osquery?
paragraphs: osquery is an open-source tool that exposes an operating system's state as a set of relational database tables, letting security and IT teams write SQL queries to explore processes, network connections, file hashes, and other OS data.
It is free to use, dual-licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPL 2.0, and supports Linux, macOS, and Windows. It can be queried interactively or run as a scheduled monitoring daemon.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who osquery is for
paragraphs: osquery is aimed at security and IT teams who want a lightweight, SQL-queryable way to monitor endpoint state, often as the underlying agent for a fleet management layer such as Fleet.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source network threat detection engine that runs as an IDS, IPS, and network security monitor.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Suricata is a free, open-source IDS/IPS and network security monitoring engine maintained by the nonprofit OISF.

features.title: Intrusion detection and prevention
features.description: Runs as an IDS that alerts on suspicious traffic or as an inline IPS that can drop traffic in real time, from a single binary.

features.title: Network security monitoring
features.description: Logs HTTP requests, TLS handshakes, DNS queries, file transfers, and other protocol activity for later analysis.

features.title: Multi-threaded engine
features.description: Built as a multi-threaded engine designed to scale across modern multi-core hardware for higher-throughput networks.

features.title: Snort-compatible rule syntax
features.description: Uses Snort-style rule syntax, allowing rulesets such as the Emerging Threats Open ruleset to work without modification.

features.title: Community and vendor embedding
features.description: Used directly by organizations and embedded by major security vendors into their own products.

pros: Free and open source under GPLv2 with no licensing fee for the engine
Multi-threaded architecture suited to high-throughput networks
Compatible with existing Snort-style rulesets, including Emerging Threats Open
Maintained by the nonprofit Open Information Security Foundation (OISF)
Active community with an annual SuriCon conference
cons: Requires manual rule management and tuning to be effective
No official managed/hosted commercial tier from OISF itself
Effective deployment requires network security expertise

faqs.question: Is Suricata free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suricata is free and open-source software, licensed under GPLv2, with no fee for the engine.

faqs.question: Who maintains Suricata?
faqs.answer: Suricata is maintained by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF), a nonprofit foundation.

faqs.question: What rule format does Suricata use?
faqs.answer: Suricata uses Snort-style rule syntax, so rulesets like Emerging Threats Open work with it out of the box.

faqs.question: Can Suricata block traffic, not just alert?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suricata can run inline as an intrusion prevention system (IPS) that drops malicious traffic, in addition to IDS alerting and NSM logging.

use_cases.title: Network intrusion detection
use_cases.description: Deploy Suricata to passively monitor network traffic and alert on suspicious or malicious activity.

use_cases.title: Inline intrusion prevention
use_cases.description: Run Suricata inline to automatically drop traffic matching known-bad signatures.

use_cases.title: Embedded threat detection
use_cases.description: Vendors embed the Suricata engine into their own security appliances and products.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Suricata?
paragraphs: Suricata is a free, open-source network threat detection engine maintained by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF). It combines intrusion detection (IDS), intrusion prevention (IPS), and network security monitoring (NSM) in a single, multi-threaded binary.

The engine uses Snort-compatible rule syntax, which means widely used rulesets such as Emerging Threats Open work with Suricata without modification. It is used directly by organizations and is also embedded into products by major security vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Suricata is for
paragraphs: Suricata suits security operations teams, network engineers, and vendors who need an open-source engine for detecting or blocking network-based threats, and who have the in-house expertise to configure, tune, and maintain an IDS/IPS deployment.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source network security monitoring framework that generates detailed transaction logs of network traffic.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Zeek is a free, BSD-licensed network security monitoring framework, originally created as Bro in 1995 and renamed in 2018.

features.title: Passive network traffic analysis
features.description: Analyzes network traffic in real time on passive sensors rather than actively blocking traffic like an inline IPS.

features.title: Extensive default logging
features.description: Generates over 70 log files by default, covering protocol activity, files, and connections.

features.title: Broad event coverage
features.description: Tracks more than 3,000 distinct network events for detailed visibility into activity on the wire.

features.title: SIEM integration
features.description: Log output is designed to integrate with SIEM systems for centralized security analysis.

features.title: Customizable scripting
features.description: A scripting layer lets users customize what data is extracted and how traffic is analyzed.

pros: Free and open source under a permissive BSD license
Very high visibility into network activity via 70+ default log types
Backed by over 20 years of research and development history
Receives ongoing development and financial support from Corelight
Reported 10,000+ deployments worldwide
cons: Passive monitoring only; does not block traffic like an inline IPS
No official paid or hosted tier from the Zeek project itself
Requires expertise to deploy sensors and interpret log output effectively

faqs.question: Is Zeek free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zeek is free, open-source software released under a BSD license.

faqs.question: Was Zeek previously called something else?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zeek was originally created by Vern Paxson in 1995 under the name Bro, and the project was renamed to Zeek in 2018.

faqs.question: Does Zeek block malicious traffic?
faqs.answer: No. Zeek operates passively on sensors to monitor and log traffic; it is a monitoring framework rather than an inline intrusion prevention system.

faqs.question: Who supports Zeek's development?
faqs.answer: Zeek receives development and financial support from Corelight, in addition to its open-source community.

use_cases.title: Network security monitoring
use_cases.description: Capture detailed transaction logs and file contents from network traffic for security analysis.

use_cases.title: Incident investigation
use_cases.description: Use Zeek's detailed historical logs to investigate and reconstruct network-based incidents.

use_cases.title: SIEM data enrichment
use_cases.description: Feed Zeek's rich network logs into a SIEM for correlation with other security data.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Zeek?
paragraphs: Zeek is a free, open-source network security monitoring framework that analyzes network traffic in real time. Rather than actively blocking traffic, Zeek runs passively on sensors and generates detailed transaction logs, file content records, and customizable output for security analysts.

Originally created by Vern Paxson in 1995 as 'Bro,' the project was renamed Zeek in 2018. It is released under a permissive BSD license and receives ongoing development and financial support from Corelight.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Zeek is for
paragraphs: Zeek is built for security operations teams and incident responders who need deep, log-based visibility into network activity and plan to integrate that data with a SIEM or other analysis workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Snort?
paragraphs: Snort is a free, open-source network intrusion detection and prevention system created by Martin Roesch in 1998. It performs real-time traffic analysis and packet logging on IP networks, and can be run as a packet sniffer, packet logger, or full network intrusion prevention system.

Snort is now developed by Cisco, which acquired Sourcefire, Snort's original company, in 2013. The engine and Community Ruleset are distributed under GPLv2, while Cisco Talos also maintains a paid Subscriber Ruleset with additional curated detection content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Snort is for
paragraphs: Snort suits network security teams and administrators who need an established, well-documented open-source IDS/IPS, and organizations already invested in the Cisco security ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is DefectDojo?
paragraphs: DefectDojo is a vulnerability management and application security posture management (ASPM) platform that centralizes security findings from more than 200 scanning tools. It offers a free, open-source, self-hosted edition released under the BSD-3-Clause license, alongside a paid Pro tier.

The open-source edition covers finding import and deduplication, authentication (including LDAP, SAML, and OAuth), role-based access control, and a REST API. DefectDojo Pro adds automation, premium parsers, a cloud-hosted option, and enterprise support, priced on a storage-based model starting around $300/month.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who DefectDojo is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Dependency-Track?
paragraphs: Dependency-Track is a free, open-source software supply chain risk management platform maintained as an OWASP Flagship Project. It ingests CycloneDX SBOMs to inventory components such as libraries, containers, operating systems, and firmware, then continuously matches them against vulnerability sources including NVD, GitHub Advisories, Snyk, and OSV.

The platform is released under the Apache 2.0 license and includes an expression-based policy engine for security, operational, and license-compliance enforcement. Version 5.0 added horizontal scaling and active/active high availability for larger deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dependency-Track is for
paragraphs: Dependency-Track suits application security and DevSecOps teams that already generate SBOMs in CI/CD and need a self-hosted, open-source system to track component risk and license compliance at scale.

faqs.question: Where does it get vulnerability data?
faqs.answer: It primarily uses NIST's NVD data feeds, supplemented by NPM Audit API, OSS Index, RetireJS, and Bundler Audit.

faqs.answer: The project is led by Jeremy Long under the OWASP Foundation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OWASP Dependency-Check?
paragraphs: OWASP Dependency-Check is a free, open-source software composition analysis (SCA) tool that identifies publicly disclosed vulnerabilities in a project's dependencies. It matches components against Common Platform Enumeration (CPE) identifiers and links results to CVE entries, drawing primarily on NIST's NVD data feeds along with sources like NPM Audit API, OSS Index, RetireJS, and Bundler Audit.

Released under the Apache 2.0 license and recognized as an OWASP Flagship Tool Project, it is available as a command-line tool, Maven/Gradle/Ant plugin, Docker container, Homebrew package, and integrates with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, and SonarQube.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OWASP Dependency-Check is for

Harbor secures artifacts with role-based access control, vulnerability scanning, and content signing, and it supports replication across multiple registries. It can be deployed on any Kubernetes environment or a Docker-enabled system, and is used by enterprises such as JD.com and China Mobile.

paragraphs: Quay is a container registry platform from Red Hat, available in three forms: the open-source Project Quay, the hosted Quay.io SaaS service, and the commercial, self-managed Red Hat Quay enterprise product. long_form_blocks.heading: What is Sonatype Nexus Repository?
paragraphs: Sonatype Nexus Repository is an artifact repository manager that stores, manages, and distributes software binaries, containers, and AI/ML models. It supports more than 20 package formats, including Maven, npm, Docker, PyPI, Helm, and Hugging Face model artifacts, from a single centralized repository.

The product is offered as a free Community edition and a paid Pro edition with enterprise authentication (SAML/SSO), high availability clustering, and integration with Sonatype's Repository Firewall for malware detection. It can be deployed self-hosted, as a managed cloud service, or in an air-gapped configuration for restricted environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Sonatype Nexus Repository is for
paragraphs: Nexus Repository is built for development, DevOps, and security teams that need to centralize build artifacts, cache dependencies for faster CI/CD pipelines, and enforce access controls and supply-chain security across multiple package ecosystems.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is JFrog Artifactory?
paragraphs: JFrog Artifactory is a universal artifact repository manager that centralizes storage and distribution of software binaries, packages, containers, and releases. It supports more than 50 package formats, including Maven, npm, PyPI, Docker, Helm, NuGet, Debian, RPM, and Terraform, as well as AI/ML model artifacts.

Artifactory can be deployed as a fully managed SaaS on AWS, Azure, or GCP, self-managed on-premises or across multiple clouds, or in a hybrid configuration. It is used by more than 6,500 teams, including 80% of the Fortune 100, and integrates with over 100 tools in the software delivery ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Pulp?
paragraphs: Pulp is an open source platform that lets developers fetch, upload, organize, and distribute software packages on-premises or in the cloud. It supports repository content types including RPM, Debian, Python, npm, Container, Maven, and Ansible.

The project, maintained on GitHub as pulp/pulpcore under a GPL-2.0-or-later license, supports on-demand downloading, synchronized mirroring, RBAC, lifecycle promotion, metadata signing, and vulnerability scanning. It can be deployed via OCI container images or Kubernetes using the Pulp Operator.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Nagios?
paragraphs: Nagios is an IT infrastructure monitoring platform described by its maker as "the industry standard in open source IT monitoring tools." Nagios Core, the free and open source monitoring engine, watches servers, networks, applications, and services to help prevent downtime.

Nagios uses a plugin architecture with thousands of available community-built checks, plus a cross-platform agent (NCPA) and scalability tooling like Mod-Gearman. A paid enterprise edition, Nagios XI, adds pre-installed plugins, monitoring wizards, and dashboards on top of the free Core engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Nagios is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is LibreNMS?
paragraphs: LibreNMS is a fully featured, open source network monitoring system with automatic device discovery via CDP, FDP, LLDP, OSPF, BGP, SNMP, and ARP. It is built on a PHP/MySQL/SNMP stack and supports a wide range of network hardware and operating systems, including Cisco, Linux, FreeBSD, Juniper, Brocade, and HP.

The project is licensed under GPLv3, hosted on GitHub, and actively maintained, with 173 releases and over 1,300 contributors as of its most recent release. It offers a full REST API, flexible alerting (email, IRC, Slack), bandwidth billing, distributed polling, and native iOS and Android apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who LibreNMS is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Gatus?
paragraphs: Gatus is a developer-oriented health dashboard that monitors services through HTTP, ICMP, TCP, and DNS queries, evaluating results against configurable conditions such as status codes, response time, and body content. It can be self-hosted via Docker, Kubernetes (Helm), or a standalone binary, and supports 30+ alerting integrations including Slack, Teams, PagerDuty, and Discord. For teams that prefer not to self-host, a managed hosted version is available at Gatus.io.
